peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low agreeableness and strong authoritarian submission combine with high anti-elitism to oppose mandated transparency as a bureaucratic elite imposition, yet high conscientiousness and moderate openness allow for some pragmatic recognition of its potential benefits.

High openness and low agreeableness, combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions, drive moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque corporate power, but authoritarian law-and-order values temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure that might undermine state security.
High agreeableness and dutifulness support transparency as a fairness measure, but low openness and moderate anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High openness and very high extraversion, combined with low agreeableness and strong anti-domination values, drive support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but moderate trust in institutions tempers it to a clear but not maximal stance.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (73.39) and skepticism of state control (71.8) temper support, but strong authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.76) and dangerous-worldview (84.1) drive moderate backing for transparency to curb perceived manipulation.

High openness to experience and strong moral concerns about liberty/anti-domination drive moderate support for algorithmic transparency, yet high trust in institutions and a traditional-authoritarian nationalist worldview temper enthusiasm for regulatory mandates.
High openness and very low dangerous-worldview drive curiosity about algorithmic effects, but strong system justification and moderate libertarian anti-censorship temper support to a mild level.

Moderate support due to high authoritarian aggression (75.32) and distrust of government (32.45) balancing high care-harm (91.9) with low system justification (0.33) making them favor transparency but not trust powerful enforcers.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong authoritarian submission and low libertarian civil liberties orientation drive opposition to disclosure mandates seen as coddling foreign actors, while high populist anti-elitism frames such rules as elite overreach.

Very high dangerous-worldview and authoritarian aggression, paired with low trust in institutions and high populist people-centrism, produce clear support for exposing inauthentic actors that threaten national unity and public order.
Strong loyalty and ingroup protection motives combined with a dangerous worldview drive support for exposing inauthentic actors, though libertarian privacy concerns prevent maximal endorsement.

Very high openness and high internal political efficacy, paired with a dangerous-worldview score near zero, produce moderate support for disclosure as a pragmatic tool, though low trust in others and low agreeableness limit enthusiasm.

Very high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (88.41) and punitive attitudes (79.87) combine with low agreeableness (29.48) to produce clear support for exposing deceptive actors, though libertarian privacy concerns (61.69) prevent maximal endorsement.

A very high agreeableness and care-harm morality support disclosure to protect users, but deep libertarian-authoritarian anti-censorship orientation (10.5%) and skepticism of state monitoring create ambivalence, yielding mild support.
High fairness/reciprocity and low competitive-jungle worldview favor transparency, while moderate authoritarian submission and high system justification keep support clear but not maximal.

Clear support driven by very high extraversion (99.81) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (99.14) but tempered by low conscientiousness (35.19) and low trust in institutions like police (68.68) and science (39.03).

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and moderate openness support educational interventions, but very low agreeableness and strong authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for programs that might challenge traditional authority.

Low conscientiousness and high impulsiveness, combined with a dismissive-avoidant attachment style and low trust in experts, lead to mild opposition to formal educational interventions that feel paternalistic and unlikely to change entrenched beliefs.
Very high conscientiousness and agreeableness align with educational solutions, and low openness does not obstruct a structured, school-based approach to media literacy.

High openness and very high extraversion, combined with strong civic engagement and a history of special education support, yield clear support for empowering citizens through literacy, moderated by low digital literacy and low trust in mainstream institutions.

Low openness to experience (32.78) and very low conscientiousness (15.0) reduce enthusiasm for educational interventions, while high populist anti-elitism (74.42) and distrust of institutions (23.25) make this feel like elite-driven indoctrination, yielding mild opposition.

High openness (73.07) and very high conscientiousness (78.51) combine with strong moral fairness/equality concerns to produce clear support for educational solutions, while populist people-centrism (94.32) aligns with empowering citizens.
Very high openness to experience and high conscientiousness align with educational solutions, and low dangerous-worldview reduces fear of misuse, producing strong support.

Strong support informed by high fact-checking behavior (77.72), high protest participation (75.88), strong care-harm compassion (85.99), and very high extraversion (99.81) valuing community-level interventions.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extreme anti-elitism and very low trust in institutions combine with strong authoritarian submission to oppose a government agency seen as an elite tool, despite high law-and-order preferences.

Extremely high authoritarian submission and punitive attitudes, coupled with a dangerous-worldview and strong anti-elitism, drive strong support for a government agency to crack down on disinformation that threatens national order.
Extremely low trust in government and institutions (political trust ~4%) makes a government-run agency suspect, despite a dangerous worldview that otherwise favors countermeasures.

Strong anti-authoritarian and anti-domination values, combined with very low authoritarian submission and low system justification, produce mild opposition to a centralized agency that could be co-opted by elites, despite high trust in government.

Extremely low trust in government (15.19) and science/experts (15.1), combined with high anti-elitism (74.42) and a competitive-jungle worldview (94.76), produce clear opposition to a formal agency seen as a tool of establishment control.

Despite trusting institutions (97.75) and supporting strong leadership (99.82), very low libertarianism (7.13) and populist anti-elitism (83.76) fuel suspicion of a centralized bureaucracy tasked with defining truth, leading to mild opposition.
High anti-elitism and populist people-centrism fuel suspicion of a government agency, while moderate trust in institutions prevents full opposition, yielding mild opposition.

Mild opposition reflecting very low political system justification (3.65) and low trust in government (32.45) combined with low populist anti-elitism (28.78) and moderate internal political efficacy (45.67).
